title: The Detached HEAD Mystery
intro: You've been experimenting with your project and suddenly Git says you're in 'detached HEAD' state. It's like wandering off the marked trail in a forest -- you can still explore, but if you don't mark your path, you won't find your way back. Commits you make here could be lost forever! Navigate this tricky situation without losing your work.

teach.title: What Is HEAD?
teach.body: In Git, **HEAD** is just a label that says "you are here." It normally points to the branch you're on (like `main`). When you make a commit, HEAD moves forward along with the branch. It's like a bookmark that follows you as you read through a book.

teach.title: Detached HEAD = Wandering Off the Trail
teach.body: Imagine you're hiking on a marked trail (your branch). You decide to step off the trail to explore an interesting rock formation (an old commit). You're free to look around, but if you build something out there and walk back to the trail, your creation gets left behind with no path back to it. That's **detached HEAD** -- you're off-branch, and new commits have no branch to anchor them.

teach.title: How Does This Happen?
teach.body:
Detached HEAD usually happens when you:

- Check out a specific commit hash: `git checkout a1b2c3d`
- Check out a tag: `git checkout v1.0`

Git shows a scary warning, but nothing is broken. You can look around safely. The danger is only if you **make new commits** here -- they could be lost when you switch back to a branch.

teach.title: Detached HEAD = Writing on a Napkin
teach.body: Imagine you're at a restaurant and have a brilliant idea. You scribble it on a napkin. If you put that napkin in your pocket (create a branch), you'll have it forever. But if you leave it on the table and walk away (switch branches without saving), the napkin gets thrown away when the table is cleared. Detached HEAD commits are napkin notes -- brilliant but fragile unless you pocket them.

teach.title: The Rescue Move
teach.code:
# If you're in detached HEAD and want to keep your work:
git checkout -b rescue-branch

# This creates a new branch right where you are,
# anchoring your commits so they won't be lost.
teach.annotation: The fix is simple: just create a branch. This gives your commits a home so Git won't garbage-collect them.

teach.body: Knowing how to escape detached HEAD without losing work is an advanced skill that impresses in technical interviews. It shows you truly understand Git -- and developers who understand their tools deeply command higher salaries and better freelance rates.
Detached HEAD means you're not on any branch -- if you make commits here, save them immediately by creating a new branch with `git checkout -b <name>`.

start.narrative:
You wanted to check out an old version of your project to see how something used to work. You ran 'git checkout a1b2c3d' (a specific commit hash). Now Git shows this scary warning:

'You are in detached HEAD state. You can look around, make experimental changes and commit them, and you can discard any commits you make in this state without impacting any branches.'

What does this mean?

choices.text: I'm looking at an old commit but I'm not on any branch -- any new commits could get lost
Something is broken and I need to fix my Git installation
My HEAD pointer was literally detached and I've lost data

understand-detached.narrative:
Correct! HEAD normally points to a branch (like main), which moves forward with each commit. In detached HEAD state, HEAD points directly to a commit instead. It's like standing in the middle of a timeline without being on a named branch.

While looking at this old code, you accidentally made two commits with a bug-fix you actually want to keep. What's the safest first move?

choices.text: Run 'git checkout -b rescue-branch' right here, while HEAD still points to your work
Run 'git checkout main' first, then figure out how to recover the commits later
Copy the changed files manually to my desktop as backup

not-broken.narrative: Nothing is broken! Detached HEAD is a normal Git state. It just means you're looking at a specific commit rather than the tip of a branch. While exploring you accidentally made two commits with a bug-fix you actually want to keep. What's the safest first move?

choices.text: Create a rescue branch immediately so the commits are anchored
Switch back to main first and rely on git reflog to recover the commits

no-data-loss.narrative: No data has been lost! 'Detached HEAD' sounds dramatic, but it just means HEAD points at a specific commit instead of following a branch. Now, while exploring you accidentally made two commits with a bug-fix you actually want to keep. What's the safest first move?

choices.text: Create a branch from where I am right now to anchor those commits
Stash the changes and switch back to main

save-branch.narrative: You run 'git checkout -b rescue-branch'. Git creates a new branch pointing at your detached commits, and your work is safely anchored. The branch is local-only -- nothing has been pushed yet. How do you make sure this work survives if your laptop dies tonight?

choices.text: Run 'git push -u origin rescue-branch' so the branch lives on the remote too
Tag the tip with 'git tag rescue-tag' and push the tag instead of the branch
Skip pushing -- the branch is local and that's good enough for now

integrate-branch.narrative: Your rescue work is now on the remote. The bug-fix commits were made on top of an OLD commit (a1b2c3d), so the rescue branch is far behind today's main. How do you get just the bug-fix into main without dragging along the old base?

choices.text: Use 'git cherry-pick' to apply just the bug-fix commits onto an up-to-date main
Open a PR to merge rescue-branch into main as-is and let reviewers sort it out
Hard-reset main to the rescue branch so the new commits are at the tip

prevent-recurrence.narrative: The bug-fix is in main. Now you want to make sure your future self doesn't accidentally commit in detached HEAD again. What's the most useful safeguard?

choices.text: Configure 'git config --global advice.detachedHead true' and add a shell prompt that shows 'DETACHED' in red when HEAD is detached
Write a one-page note in the team wiki that says 'don't checkout commit hashes'
Just remember to be more careful next time

end-perfect.narrative: You contained the detached HEAD, anchored your commits in a rescue branch, pushed it to the remote, cherry-picked the bug-fix onto an up-to-date main, and added a prompt indicator that warns you whenever HEAD detaches again. This won't catch you off-guard a second time.
end-perfect.feedback: Textbook recovery. The order of operations -- branch first, push second, cherry-pick third, then add a visible safeguard -- is exactly what experienced Git users do. The prompt indicator turns a confusing state into an obvious one, which is the difference between a junior and a senior git user.

end-good.narrative: You saved the work, pushed it, and cherry-picked the bug-fix into main. The wiki note will help if anyone reads it, but most teammates will hit the same issue before they ever browse the wiki.
end-good.feedback: You handled the recovery well. Documentation is fine, but it's a passive safeguard -- people don't read the wiki until after they've already broken something. A prompt indicator or a Git alias is active: it warns you in the moment. Small upgrade, big difference.

end-weak.narrative: The immediate fire is out, but nothing has changed about your workflow. The next time you check out a commit hash to look at history, you'll commit on top of it again, and only the lucky discovery of 'git reflog' will save you.
end-weak.feedback: Recovery worked, but you skipped the most important step: making sure you don't repeat the mistake. 'I'll be more careful' is not a safeguard -- it's a wish. Add a prompt indicator, an alias for safer checkouts, or a pre-commit hook that refuses commits in detached HEAD.
